planting.note: Bright light keeps the gold color vivid; some afternoon shade in hot climates.

directions: Press small cuttings onto gritty, well-drained soil where they root into a low golden mat. The tiny bright chartreuse-yellow leaves form a moss-like carpet that lights up rock gardens, containers, and green roofs. Give it good light for the strongest color, water lightly, and protect it from prolonged winter wet in cold zones.

planting.note: Native to the Pacific Northwest; likes full sun and gritty soil.

directions: Root cuttings in sharply drained, gravelly soil in a sunny position. This Pacific Northwest native forms mats of plump, bead-like green leaves that flush red in sun, with yellow star flowers in summer. It handles cold and rocky ground well, so keep it lean and water only during drought.

planting.note: A western North American native for hot, dry, gritty sites.

directions: Set divisions or surface-sow seed into sharply drained, gravelly soil in full sun. This drought-tough western native forms low rosettes of narrow, lance-shaped leaves and sends up bright yellow star flowers in early summer. Keep it dry and lean, avoiding rich soil or irrigation that causes it to rot.

planting.note: Upright species for the middle of a sunny border.

directions: Plant divisions into well-drained garden soil with the crown at the surface. Unlike the mat-forming stonecrops, this species makes upright leafy stems topped by flat clusters of yellow-orange star flowers in summer, followed by ornamental reddish seed heads. Water moderately, and cut old stems to the ground in late winter.

planting.note: Full sun and good drainage for this tidy mounding stonecrop.

directions: Set divisions into well-drained soil in full sun and firm the roots. It forms neat mounds of narrow, toothed bronze-green leaves and bears yellow star flowers in summer that give way to russet seed heads. Give it lean conditions and little water, trimming spent flower stalks to keep the mound compact.

planting.note: Tolerates light shade better than most stonecrops.

directions: Plant divisions into well-drained soil in sun or light shade. Unusual among stonecrops, it has broad, toothed, poplar-like leaves on semi-woody stems and produces fragrant pinkish-white flowers in late summer. Water moderately and cut back the somewhat shrubby stems in early spring to renew growth.

planting.note: Tolerates part shade; good between stepping stones.

directions: Press cuttings onto well-drained soil where they root along the creeping stems. Its flat, round, coin-shaped green leaves flush bronze-red in sun and cold, forming a low carpet topped with yellow star flowers in summer. It tolerates some shade and light foot traffic, so water sparingly and let it fill in around pavers.

planting.note: Full sun to light shade; protect from hard winters in colder zones.

directions: Root cuttings in well-drained soil where the trailing stems knit into a light, airy mat. The slender needle leaves are edged in creamy white, sometimes blushing pink, and yellow star flowers appear in early summer. It grows fast and spills nicely from containers and walls, needing only occasional water and a trim to shape.

planting.note: A western North American native for hot, dry, rocky sites.

directions: Set divisions or surface-sow seed into sharply drained, gravelly ground in full sun. This western native forms low tufts of slender green leaves that redden in sun and produces bright yellow star flowers in early summer. It needs excellent drainage and little water, so keep it lean and avoid overwatering.

planting.note: Full sun and good drainage; lovely spilling over walls.

directions: Plant divisions into well-drained soil where the arching stems can trail over an edge or rock. It carries small rounded blue-grey leaves on lax stems and produces dense clusters of deep rose-pink flowers in late summer and fall. Give it full sun and lean soil, cutting back the old stems in early spring.

planting.note: Full sun and sharp drainage for this trailing rock-garden sedum.

directions: Set divisions into gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. Rounded blue-green leaves clasp the trailing stems, which end in rosy-pink flower clusters in late summer that attract bees and butterflies. Keep it lean and dry, cutting the semi-woody stems back in spring for fresh growth.

planting.note: Full sun and gritty soil; evergreen in mild areas.

directions: Plant divisions into a sharply drained gritty bed in full sun. This European alpine has trailing stems of rounded blue-grey leaves that end in tight clusters of dull purple-pink summer flowers. It suits walls, troughs, and rock gardens; keep it lean and water only during drought.

planting.note: Full sun and average, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant divisions into well-drained soil with the crown at the surface. This compact, sprawling hybrid carries grey-purple foliage on low mounding stems topped with ruby-red flower clusters from late summer into fall. Water sparingly, avoid rich soil that causes flopping, and cut old stems back in late winter.

planting.note: Full sun and lean soil keep stems upright.

directions: Set divisions into well-drained garden soil and firm around the roots. Dark reddish stems and grey-green foliage support tight, dome-shaped clusters of deep red-pink flowers in late summer that resemble little cauliflowers. Grow it in full sun and lean soil to prevent flopping, and leave seed heads for winter interest.

planting.note: Full sun and well-drained soil for sturdy upright clumps.

directions: Plant divisions into well-drained soil with the crown level to the surface. This upright showy stonecrop bears fleshy blue-green foliage topped by broad flat clusters of pure white star flowers in late summer and fall. Bees love the blooms; grow it in full sun, water little, and leave the seed heads standing for winter structure.

planting.note: Full sun and lean, well-drained soil.

directions: Set divisions into well-drained soil and space to allow the clumps to fill in. Sturdy upright stems with grey-green foliage carry large flat heads of carmine-rose flowers in late summer that draw butterflies and bees. Grow it lean and sunny to keep stems from flopping, and leave the rusty seed heads for winter.

planting.note: Full sun and average soil for strong upright stems.

directions: Plant divisions into well-drained garden soil with the crown at the surface. This vigorous upright hybrid holds sturdy green-leaved stems topped by large flat clusters of bright rosy-pink flowers in late summer. It stands up well without staking; grow it in full sun, water modestly, and cut back in late winter.

planting.note: Full sun; naturally compact and mounded.

directions: Set divisions into well-drained soil and space for the tidy mounds to knit together. This compact selection forms a neat dome of blue-green foliage smothered in soft bubblegum-pink flowers in late summer that pollinators adore. It needs no staking; grow it in full sun, water sparingly, and shear old stems in spring.

planting.note: Full sun for the richest plum foliage color.

directions: Plant divisions into well-drained soil where the low, spreading habit can form a tidy mound. This SunSparkler selection carries deep plum-purple foliage topped with rosy-red flower clusters in late summer and fall. Grow it in full sun for the best leaf color, water sparingly, and trim old stems back in spring.

planting.note: Full sun deepens the chocolate foliage.

directions: Plant divisions into well-drained soil with the crown at the surface. This compact selection holds dark chocolate-brown foliage on sturdy stems topped with dusky pink flower clusters in late summer. Grow it in full sun for the richest leaf color, water modestly, and leave the seed heads for winter interest.

planting.note: Set out divisions once the soil has warmed and the last hard frost has passed.

directions: Grown mainly for its dramatic cream-margined variegated leaves topped by pale lilac flowers with a darker eye. Plant crowns level with the soil in rich, well-drained ground and keep evenly moist through summer. Thin weak stems in spring to boost airflow and cut spent panicles to tidy the clump.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the ground is workable and frost risk has eased.

directions: Bright gold-edged foliage sets off warm salmon-pink flowers with a red eye in midsummer. Give it a spot with full sun and steady moisture, and mulch to keep the roots cool. Deadhead the fading heads to encourage a lighter rebloom and improve appearance.

planting.note: Divide and plant in spring once soil temperatures are rising.

directions: Prized for large heads of lavender-blue flowers that read most blue in the cool light of evening. Plant in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and water during dry spells to prevent lower-leaf mildew. Space generously and remove finished flower clusters to keep the display fresh.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring when frost danger has passed.

directions: Opens soft blush-pink and fades toward near-white, giving a gentle two-tone effect across the panicle. Grow in full sun with rich soil and consistent water for the strongest stems. Cut back after bloom and lift and divide crowded clumps every few years to keep it vigorous.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: A long-grown Dutch selection with large domed heads of clear rose-pink flowers loved by butterflies. Plant in deep, fertile soil and water steadily so the tall stems stay upright. Deadheading spent trusses often triggers a modest second flush later in the season.

planting.note: Divide and plant in spring once the soil warms.

directions: Vivid salmon-orange flowers give this old favorite unusually warm color for a phlox. Site in full sun with good air movement and even moisture to limit mildew on the lower leaves. Remove faded heads promptly and stake if the clump is grown in richer, taller conditions.

planting.note: Set out in spring after the last frost in a sunny position.

directions: Rich violet-purple flowers on sturdy, compact stems make a saturated block of color in the border. Plant in fertile ground and keep watered through the heat of summer for full trusses. Cut the plant back after flowering to keep the clump tidy and reduce self-seeding.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun for the best mildew resistance.

directions: A tough, mildew-resistant selection bearing white flowers each marked with a small lilac eye. Grow in sun with steady moisture, and its clean foliage holds up well even in humid summers. Deadhead the tall panicles as they fade to prolong the airy white display.

planting.note: Plant compact divisions in spring near the front of a sunny bed.

directions: A shorter, well-branched phlox with pale pink flowers boldly marked by a raspberry-red eye. Its compact habit suits the front of the border and rarely needs staking. Keep the soil evenly moist and shear lightly after the first flush to encourage more bloom.

planting.note: Set out in spring in full sun once frost has passed.

directions: Deep magenta-red flowers glow above dark-tinged stems on a neat, bushy plant. Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and water regularly to keep the intense color from scorching. Remove spent heads and divide every few years to maintain flowering strength.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, fertile spot.

directions: Delicate shell-pink flowers with a slightly deeper eye give a soft, cottage-garden feel. Grow in rich soil with even moisture and space plants for good airflow to reduce mildew. Deadhead through summer and cut stems to the ground after frost blackens the foliage.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring where the variegated foliage can be admired.

directions: Striking white-margined leaves are topped by reddish-purple flowers for a bold two-part show. Grow in full sun to partial shade with steady moisture and rich soil. Remove any all-green reverted shoots at once so the variegation stays true across the clump.

planting.note: Plant tall divisions in spring in full sun with room to grow.

directions: A tall, late-blooming selection carrying large heads of pure white flowers into early fall. Plant in deep, fertile soil and water well so the strong stems stand without support. Its late season extends the phlox display after earlier cultivars finish.

planting.note: Divide and plant in spring in a sunny border.

directions: Intense cerise-magenta flowers make a vivid statement in the late-summer garden. Grow in full sun and rich soil, keeping the roots cool with mulch and steady water. Deadhead the bright panicles and thin the stems in spring for better air movement.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Crisp white flowers accented by a bright carmine-red eye create a lively bicolor effect. Site in full sun with good airflow and even moisture for clean, healthy foliage. Cut away spent trusses through the season to keep new buds forming.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring for an early-summer start.

directions: One of the earlier phlox to open, with deep carmine-red flowers on compact stems. Grow in full sun and fertile soil, watering during dry weather to keep the color strong. Deadheading after the first flush often brings a lighter repeat bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, well-prepared bed.

directions: Soft lilac flowers edged with a paler, almost white rim give a gentle luminous quality. Grow in rich soil with steady moisture and full sun for the fullest trusses. Space plants well and thin crowded stems to keep mildew at bay.

planting.note: Plant dwarf divisions in spring at the front of a sunny bed or in a container.

directions: A dwarf, compact phlox from the Flame series with fragrant clear-pink flowers. Its low mounded form is ideal for the border edge, pots, and small gardens. Keep well watered and deadhead to extend the long midsummer show.

planting.note: Plant dwarf divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: A short, tidy Flame-series phlox with fragrant purple flowers on a rounded plant. Its dwarf habit rarely needs staking and suits containers and front borders. Provide steady moisture and remove spent heads to prolong flowering.

planting.note: Plant dwarf divisions in spring in a sunny spot or container.

directions: A compact Flame-series phlox bearing fragrant pure-white flowers on a low, bushy mound. It brightens the front of the border and glows in evening light. Keep evenly moist and deadhead through summer for continued bloom.

planting.note: Plant compact divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: A shorter, well-branched selection loaded with clean white flowers over healthy green foliage. Its compact habit and good mildew resistance make it easy in mixed borders. Water steadily and remove faded heads to keep the fresh white display going.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun after frost.

directions: Bright bubblegum-pink flowers cover a compact, mildew-resistant plant through midsummer. Grow in full sun with fertile soil and even moisture for the best flower size. Deadhead spent trusses and divide clumps every few years to keep them vigorous.

planting.note: Plant dwarf divisions in spring near the front of a sunny bed.

directions: A dwarf phlox smothered in bright cerise-red flowers on a compact, weather-tough plant. Its low habit suits containers and border fronts and needs no staking. Keep evenly moist and shear lightly after the first flush for repeat bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, sheltered spot.

directions: An unusual novelty phlox whose feathery heads mix soft green and pink in a shaggy, informal spray. Grow in full sun and rich soil with even moisture for the fullest plumes. Leave the curious heads standing well into fall, then cut back after frost.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Lavender-blue flowers deepen to a true blue tone as evening light fades. Plant in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and keep watered to avoid lower-leaf mildew. Deadhead through summer and divide crowded clumps in spring to sustain flowering.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny border.

directions: Large pale-pink flowers with a bold cerise eye sit on tall, strong stems. Grow in full sun with rich soil and consistent water for big, showy trusses. Remove spent heads to keep it flowering and cut down the stems after frost.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Each pale flower is striped with magenta rays that fan out like spokes on a wheel. Grow in full sun and fertile soil with even moisture for the crispest markings. Deadhead through summer and give good airflow to keep the foliage clean.

planting.note: Plant dwarf divisions in spring in full sun or a container.

directions: A very compact phlox with purple-pink flowers marked by a paler center on a low mound. Its dwarf, spreading habit is well suited to pots and rock-garden edges. Keep the soil evenly moist and trim lightly after bloom to keep it neat.

planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted pieces in spring on a sunny, well-drained slope.

directions: A low, spreading mat of needle foliage covered in white flowers with a small pink-purple eye each spring. Plant in lean, sharply drained soil in full sun where it can carpet banks and rock walls. Shear lightly after bloom to keep the cushion dense and tidy.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: A pure-white creeping phlox (its German name means May snow) that blankets the mat in spring. Grow in full sun with excellent drainage and it tolerates heat and drought once settled. Trim back after flowering to encourage tight, fresh growth.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun on well-drained ground.

directions: Soft lavender-blue flowers cover this dense creeping mat in mid-spring. Plant in lean, gritty soil in full sun where it can spill over walls or edge a path. Shear after bloom and it needs little water once established.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, sharply drained spot.

directions: An old, reliable selection with clear lilac-blue flowers over fine evergreen foliage. Grow in full sun and gritty soil where it forms a spreading spring carpet. Clip lightly after flowering to keep the mat compact and free of bare patches.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Soft pale-pink flowers with a slightly deeper eye give a gentle apple-blossom look in spring. Plant in lean, well-drained soil in full sun to carpet slopes and borders. Shear back after bloom to maintain a tight, healthy cushion.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, gritty bed or rock garden.

directions: A choice creeping phlox with bright rose-pink flowers set off by a small deeper eye. Its neat, low habit suits rock gardens, troughs, and wall tops in full sun. Give sharp drainage, avoid soggy soil, and shear after bloom for density.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in humus-rich soil in dappled shade.

directions: A woodland creeper spreading by runners to form a low carpet of bright rose-pink spring flowers. Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade beneath shrubs or trees. Keep the soil from drying out and let the stolons knit into a groundcover.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in rich, moist woodland soil in partial shade.

directions: White flowers with a yellow eye light up shady beds in mid-spring on a spreading woodland mat. Plant in humus-rich, evenly moist soil in partial to full shade. Allow the creeping stems to root and colonize as a fragrant groundcover.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in shaded, humus-rich ground.

directions: Fragrant lavender-blue flowers cover this spreading woodland phlox in spring. Grow in moist, rich soil in partial shade where it makes an easy groundcover. Keep the soil evenly damp and let the runners fill in bare ground over time.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in rich, moist soil in partial shade.

directions: Softly fragrant flowers open palest ice-blue and fade nearly white above loose spring mounds. Plant in humus-rich, evenly moist woodland soil in dappled shade. Cut the stems back after bloom to freshen the foliage and limit mildew.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, shaded, humus-rich soil.

directions: A compact woodland phlox bearing clean white flowers with notched petals in mid-spring. Grow in rich, evenly moist soil in partial shade among ferns and spring bulbs. Its low, mildew-resistant habit spreads gently to form a fragrant patch.

planting.note: Set out young clumps once hard frosts have passed and soil has warmed.

directions: Plant in gritty, well-drained soil with the crown level at the surface, giving each rosette room to form its low mound of sage-green, quilted leaves. It resents winter wet, so a lean site in full sun suits it best. Pale bluish-white flowers veined in violet open in early summer above the foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring so the sprawling stems establish before summer flowering.

directions: Set plants in moist but free-draining soil where their loose, scrambling stems can weave through neighbors or tumble over a bank. Trim back tired growth in midsummer to spark a fresh flush of bloom. Saucer-shaped flowers with pale centers appear from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring as new growth begins.

directions: This sterile hybrid is grown from divisions, so plant a rooted clump with its crown at soil level and mulch to conserve moisture. It forms a broad, spreading mound covered in violet-blue, white-eyed flowers from early summer to frost. Shear lightly after the first heavy flush to keep it tidy and blooming.

planting.note: Plant after frost danger passes into a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Set this low, trailing selection at the front of a border or in a container where its bronze-tinted foliage can spill over an edge. Cherry-pink flowers with pale centers cover the plant from early summer well into autumn. Trim spent stems occasionally to keep flowering strong.

planting.note: Plant in spring in any reasonable garden soil.

directions: Plant this tough, clump-forming species in ordinary soil where it will tolerate both sun and light shade. Deep violet-blue flowers with darker veins rise above hairy, wrinkled leaves in early summer. Cut the whole plant back after flowering to refresh the foliage.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in sun or light shade.

directions: Grow this vigorous hybrid from divisions set in fertile, well-drained soil where it can knit into a broad, airy mound. Finely cut leaves emerge yellowish and mature to green, topped by lilac-blue, dark-veined flowers through summer. Shear back after the main flush to encourage a second show.

planting.note: Set out clumps in spring in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Plant this free-flowering hybrid in good soil and full sun for the strongest display. Large, upward-facing pink flowers with fine darker veins bloom over a long midsummer season. Deadhead or shear lightly to prolong the show and keep the mound compact.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: This renardii hybrid inherits handsome grey-green, quilted foliage that forms a neat low mound. Give it lean, sharply drained soil in full sun and avoid winter wet. Violet-blue flowers with bold dark veining open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring where trailing stems can scramble through other plants.

directions: Set this scrambling hybrid where its long, weaving stems can thread up through shrubs and other perennials. Yellow-flushed young leaves set off dusky violet-pink flowers with dark centers and veins over a long summer season. Cut it back when it outgrows its space to keep it flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a spot with some afternoon shade in hot climates.

directions: This trailing hybrid opens the season with bright golden foliage that mellows to green as summer heat arrives. Blue-violet flowers with reddish centers bloom for months above the spreading mound. Site it out of the hottest afternoon sun to protect the colorful young leaves from scorch.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained, fertile soil.

directions: Plant this long-blooming hybrid where its low, spreading stems can fill gaps at the front of a bed. Flowers open purple-pink with a paler center and violet veining, shifting shades as they age, and continue from early summer to autumn. Trim after the first flush to keep the plant fresh.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun and average, well-drained soil.

directions: This early-flowering hybrid produces large violet-blue blooms with dark veins from late spring. Plant it in full sun in ordinary garden soil and cut it back after the first flush to prompt repeat bloom. It makes a strong, upright clump in the mid-border.

planting.note: Plant after frosts into warm, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant this low, sprawling hybrid where its trailing stems can carpet the ground or drape over a wall. Grey-green leaves back magenta-pink flowers that appear all summer long. It flowers best in full sun and free-draining soil, and can be trimmed to control its spread.

planting.note: Plant in warm, well-drained soil after the last frost.

directions: Set this trailing selection where its silvery-green stems can spread into a wide, low mat. Soft pink flowers with paler centers bloom continuously through the summer months. Give it sun and sharp drainage, and shear back any overreaching stems as needed.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun or partial shade in ordinary soil.

directions: This dependable old hybrid forms a dense, weed-smothering clump topped with silvery-pink flowers over a long season. Plant it in almost any soil in sun or light shade and shear it back after the first flush for fresh foliage and more bloom. It is an easy, adaptable ground cover.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun or partial shade.

directions: Plant this vigorous hybrid in ordinary garden soil where it will form a substantial leafy clump. Soft rose-pink flowers with darker veining appear over a long summer season. Cut it hard after flowering to renew the foliage and encourage more blooms.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun or partial shade in average soil.

directions: The chief attraction of this hybrid is its foliage, boldly marked with a reddish-brown zone at the leaf center. Pale pink, darker-veined flowers rise above the clump through summer. Plant in ordinary soil and cut back after flowering to bring on a fresh flush of the marked leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun or partial shade.

directions: This curious form bears narrow, strap-like pinkish-purple petals that give the flowers a distinctive starry, almost spidery look. Grow it as an easy, spreading clump in ordinary soil in sun or light shade. Shear after the first flowering to tidy the plant and prompt repeat bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: This seed strain emerges with smoky, purple-flushed foliage that sets off its blue-violet early-summer flowers. Plant in fertile soil in full sun and stake taller stems in exposed sites. Cut the whole plant to the ground after flowering for a fresh mound of colored leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: This dwarf selection is prized for its deep purple-black, finely divided foliage on a compact mound. Small violet-blue flowers appear in early summer, but the leaves are the main draw. Plant in full sun for the strongest leaf color and avoid crowding it under taller neighbors.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, moist soil in shade or part shade.

directions: This robust dusky cranesbill carries large rich mauve-purple flowers on tall stems in late spring. It thrives in shade or partial shade in cool, moisture-retentive soil, making it valuable for woodland edges. Cut back after flowering to freshen the light green foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade or part shade in moist soil.

directions: Grow this dusky cranesbill for its deep, dark maroon-purple flowers held on wiry stems in late spring. It performs well in shade and cool, moist soil where many perennials struggle. Deadhead or cut back after bloom to keep the clump tidy and prevent excess seedlings.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade or part shade in moist soil.

directions: This selection is grown as much for its foliage as its flowers, with new leaves splashed cream and yellow that green up as the season warms. Dusky purple flowers rise above the variegated mound in late spring. Site it in shade to protect the young leaf coloring from scorch.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; tolerates sun to shade and dry soil.

directions: This tough bigroot selection forms a weed-smothering carpet of aromatic foliage that colors well in autumn. Deep magenta-pink flowers appear in early summer above the leaves. It thrives in dry shade under trees and needs little care once established.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in sun to shade, tolerating dry soil.

directions: A low, spreading bigroot cranesbill bearing pale pink to nearly white flowers in early summer. The aromatic foliage forms dense ground cover and takes on red tints in autumn. Plant it in dry shade or sun and divide clumps when they become crowded.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade to protect the variegation.

directions: This selection carries aromatic leaves irregularly margined and splashed with creamy yellow. Magenta-pink flowers appear in early summer above the variegated mound. Grow it in part shade for the best leaf color and remove any all-green shoots that appear.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: This white-flowered form of the bloody cranesbill makes a loose, mounding clump of finely cut leaves. Pure white flowers open from early to midsummer and the foliage often reddens in autumn. Plant in full sun and free-draining soil, where it is drought-tolerant once established.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: A compact bloody cranesbill with bright pink flowers edged in a paler, silvery rim over a very long season. It forms a low, dense mat ideal for edging or the rock garden. Plant in full sun and lean, well-drained soil and shear lightly if it sprawls.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: This selection bears unusually large, rich magenta flowers over the typical finely cut foliage. It forms a spreading mound that flowers heavily in early summer with scattered later bloom. Grow in full sun and free-draining soil and trim after the main flush to tidy the plant.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, sharply drained soil in full sun.

directions: This compact alpine cranesbill forms a tight cushion of grey-green leaves studded with purple-red, dark-veined flowers. It is well suited to rock gardens, troughs, and the front of a sunny bed. Plant in gritty, sharply drained soil and avoid winter wet around the crown.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: A low, spreading alpine cranesbill with pale pink flowers boldly marked by darker magenta veins and a deep central eye. It suits rock gardens and sunny edges where drainage is sharp. Plant in gritty soil and trim spent flowers to encourage a longer display.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, fertile soil in sun or part shade.

directions: This wood cranesbill blooms early, carrying rich violet-blue flowers with white centers on upright stems in late spring. Plant it in moist, fertile soil in sun or dappled shade, where it naturalizes well at a woodland edge. Cut back after flowering to keep the foliage fresh.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, fertile soil in sun or part shade.

directions: The white-flowered wood cranesbill lights up a shady border with pure white blooms in late spring. Plant it in cool, moisture-retentive soil in partial shade for the longest-lasting flowers. Deadhead or cut back after bloom to tidy the clump and limit self-seeding.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, fertile soil in sun or part shade.

directions: This selection bears soft pink flowers finely veined in a deeper shade above deeply cut foliage. It spreads by rhizomes to form a light, open colony and blooms through early to midsummer. Plant in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and cut back after flowering to refresh the clump.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun or part shade; tolerates dry soil once established.

directions: This low, spreading hybrid forms a dense mat of glossy, aromatic foliage that colors in autumn. Bright purplish-pink flowers cover the plant in early summer. It makes excellent ground cover in sun or light shade and asks for little once established.

planting.note: Plant out only after all frost danger has passed in mild-winter regions.

directions: This giant, tender cranesbill builds a huge rosette of deeply cut leaves over a year or two, then throws up a spectacular dome of magenta-pink flowers before dying. Grow it in frost-free gardens or as a container plant moved under cover for winter. Leave the old leaf stalks in place, as they help prop up the towering flower stem.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in mild regions or grow in a sheltered spot.

directions: This bold, near-shrubby cranesbill forms a large rosette of glossy, deeply divided leaves and sends up airy stems of bright purplish-pink flowers in summer. It is only half-hardy, so grow it in a sheltered, mild-winter spot or overwinter young plants under cover. It self-seeds freely where happy, providing replacements as older rosettes fade.

planting.note: Plant tubers in autumn a few inches deep in free-draining soil.

directions: Plant the small tubers in autumn about three inches deep in a sunny, sharply drained spot. Deeply cut leaves and lilac-pink, dark-veined flowers appear in spring, then the plant dies back and rests underground through the dry summer. It is ideal for a gravel garden or naturalizing in a warm, well-drained bank.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, fertile soil in sun or part shade.

directions: Grow this species as much for its foliage, which emerges soft and downy, flushes bronze, and turns rich red in autumn. Dusky violet flowers with pale centers appear through summer. Plant in moisture-retentive soil in sun or light shade for the best fall color.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist soil in sun or part shade.

directions: This species is named for its white flowers finely pencilled with a network of bright magenta veins. It forms a loose clump in sun or light shade and blooms from late spring into summer. Plant in moisture-retentive soil and cut back after flowering, as it can self-seed generously.

planting.note: Plant out after frost when the ground is thoroughly warm.

directions: Grow from rooted cuttings and place in rich soil with steady moisture through the heat of summer. The chartreuse-gold foliage glows all season and gives way to crimson autumn flowers on tall stems. A little afternoon shade in the hottest regions keeps the bright leaves from scorching.

planting.note: Sow in spring for a rosette the first year and flowers the next.

directions: Sow just beneath the soil surface and grow in gritty, sharply drained ground, as this species rots in wet winter soil. The first year forms a broad rosette of felted, silvery-white leaves prized as a foliage accent, followed by branching stalks of white flowers in year two. Removing the bloom stalk early can prolong the striking foliage rosette for another season.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall onto a firmed seedbed.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface and press in, since light aids germination for this eastern North American native. Basal rosettes of lobed leaves send up airy spikes of pale lavender-blue flowers in spring, and plants self-sow to form a loose groundcover. It tolerates lean, moist soil and light shade at a woodland edge.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring in sun or light shade.

directions: Plant this selection for its deep burgundy-purple rosettes, which color best with several hours of direct sun. Thin spikes of pale blue-white flowers rise in spring above the dark foliage. Divide established clumps or let seedlings fill in, thinning any that revert to plain green.

planting.note: Plant out after frost into warm, fertile soil.

directions: Grow from cuttings and give it space, rich soil, and full sun to fuel its long season of deep purple flowers held in near-black calyces. It blooms from early summer until frost with almost no deadheading and draws hummingbirds constantly. In colder zones lift or take cuttings before winter, since the roots survive only where the ground stays mild.

planting.note: Set out after frost in warm ground.

directions: This sterile hybrid is grown only from cuttings and rewards rich soil and steady feeding with magenta-pink tubular flowers on wine-red stems. It flowers continuously from late spring to frost and is a magnet for hummingbirds and bees. Give it a sheltered, sunny spot and cut back hard if growth flops in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant out after all danger of frost.

directions: A relative of Wendy's Wish, this cutting-grown hybrid carries deep burgundy-purple flowers from dark maroon calyces on nearly black stems. It blooms tirelessly through the warm months and works well in large containers as well as borders. Feed regularly, water in dry spells, and shear lightly to keep it dense.

planting.note: Plant out after frost into warm, rich soil.

directions: Unlike its cobalt relatives, this selection bears soft sky-blue flowers on tall, upright stems from summer into fall. Grow from cuttings or divisions in fertile ground with room to reach chest height. Mulch the crown heavily where winters are cold, since the tuberous roots may return in borderline zones.

planting.note: Set out after frost when soil is warm.

directions: This vigorous selection carries rich purple flowers from dark calyces over a long summer season and spreads politely by rhizomes. Grow it from cuttings in full sun and average soil, watering during heat. Cut spent stems to keep it flowering, and mulch the roots for winter in zone 7.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once soil is workable.

directions: This clean white form of woodland sage forms a tidy clump topped with slender spikes over aromatic foliage. Plant divisions in full sun and average, well-drained soil, then shear after the first flush to bring a second bloom. It is drought tolerant once established and never needs staking.

planting.note: Plant after frost in sharply drained soil.

directions: Grow this Navajo-series autumn sage from cuttings in gritty, fast-draining soil and full sun. Clear true-red flowers appear from late spring through fall and draw hummingbirds. Shear the shrub by a third in midsummer to renew the flush, and avoid overwatering.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a hot, well-drained spot.

directions: This vigorous, floriferous selection bears bright coral-pink flowers over an exceptionally long season. Grow from cuttings in lean, dry soil and full sun, giving it room to reach a broad, open form. Trim lightly after heavy flushes to keep it tidy and blooming into autumn.

planting.note: Set out after frost in fast-draining ground.

directions: Plant this cutting-grown autumn sage for its deep cherry-red flowers held above small aromatic leaves. It thrives in heat, poor soil, and full sun with little water once established. Cut back by a third after the first bloom flush to keep flowers coming until frost.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a sunny, dry bed.

directions: A compact autumn sage bearing vivid, saturated red flowers over a tidy mound of small leaves. Grow from cuttings in gritty, well-drained soil and full sun, watering sparingly. Shear after flushes to maintain shape and prolong the long bloom season that hummingbirds favor.

planting.note: Plant after frost in well-drained soil.

directions: This robust baby sage forms a large, open shrub covered in glowing magenta-pink flowers from summer to frost. Grow from cuttings in full sun and free-draining soil, allowing plenty of room to spread. Prune hard in early spring to renew the framework and remove any winter-damaged wood.

planting.note: Set out after frost in a warm, sunny spot.

directions: Grown from cuttings, this baby sage bears large, deep rose-pink flowers over a long warm-season display. Give it full sun and lean, well-drained soil, and it will flower reliably until the first frost. Cut back in spring and trim after heavy flushes to keep it bushy.

planting.note: Plant after frost in fast-draining soil.

directions: This jame hybrid produces rich raspberry-red flowers continuously through the warm months on a compact shrubby plant. Grow from cuttings in full sun and gritty soil, watering only in prolonged dry spells. Shear lightly after flushes and prune the framework in spring.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in a sunny, dry bed.

directions: Warm apricot-orange flowers give this jame hybrid its name, blooming from late spring to frost on a tidy shrub. Grow from cuttings in full sun and sharply drained soil, keeping water minimal. Trim after flushes for continued bloom and prune the plant in early spring.

planting.note: Set out after frost in well-drained ground.

directions: This subtle jame hybrid opens soft cream-and-pink flowers that shift shade with temperature over a long season. Grow from cuttings in full sun and lean, free-draining soil with little supplemental water. Shear after flushes and cut back the framework in spring for dense growth.

planting.note: Plant after frost in warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Also sold as Purple Velvet, this selection carries solid deep-purple flower spikes rather than the usual white-and-purple bicolor. Grow from cuttings in full sun and well-drained soil, giving the arching stems ample room. The velvety flowers open in late summer and fall, so hold off on hard pruning until spring in mild zones.

planting.note: Transplant after frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Start seed indoors eight to ten weeks before your last frost and set plants out into warm soil. Silvery-white flower spikes rise above gray-green foliage all summer and are excellent for cutting and drying. Deadhead or shear spent spikes to keep the display going, and it may return as a short-lived perennial in mild zones.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun.

directions: The white-flowered counterpart to Henry Duelberg, this tough Texas selection forms a large, drought-hardy clump of tall white spikes. Grow from cuttings or divisions in full sun and average to poor soil, where it blooms from spring through fall. Cut back after the first heavy flush to encourage a strong rebloom.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into warm soil.

directions: This compact, award-winning tropical sage blooms early from seed and flowers nonstop all summer. Start indoors six to eight weeks ahead or sow directly once soil warms, then set plants in full sun. The dense spikes of small red flowers draw hummingbirds and butterflies and need almost no deadheading.

planting.note: Set out after frost when soil is warm.

directions: A soft-pink form of the popular Summer Jewel series, quick to flower from seed and blooming continuously into fall. Start seed indoors or direct sow after frost and grow in full sun with average water. The tidy plants self-clean and pull in bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into warm ground.

directions: This pure-white tropical sage flowers all summer and self-sows freely to return year after year in mild gardens. Start indoors or sow directly after frost and grow in full sun with average, well-drained soil. Shear midseason if it gets leggy, and expect hummingbirds and butterflies at the flowers.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and average soil.

directions: This clump-forming sage bears airy spikes ringed with whorls of small lilac-violet flowers over coarse, aromatic foliage. Grow in full sun and well-drained soil, then shear the spent spikes for a long, repeating bloom. It tolerates drought and lean ground and self-sows lightly where happy.

planting.note: Set out after frost in rich, moist soil.

directions: This large-flowered form of gentian sage bears exceptionally big, deep-blue hooded flowers on a tall, upright plant. Start seed indoors and grow in fertile, moisture-retentive soil in full sun to light shade. In cold zones lift the tuberous roots for winter storage, much like a dahlia.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade and humus-rich soil.

directions: This woodland sage tolerates shade and bears soft primrose-yellow flowers marked with maroon on sticky, arrow-shaped foliage. Grow in moist, humus-rich soil under dappled light, where it blooms from late summer into autumn. It self-sows gently and suits a shaded border or woodland edge.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun or light shade.

directions: This clump-forming sage makes a bold rosette of large, rough basal leaves and sends up branching spikes of violet-blue flowers with paler markings. Grow in average, well-drained soil in full sun to light shade, where it flowers through early summer. Cut spent stems to encourage more bloom, and divide congested clumps in spring.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in warm, moist soil.

directions: Named for its distinctive triangular leaves, this Central American sage bears clear gentian-blue flowers over a long warm season. Grow from cuttings in fertile, moisture-retentive soil with light shade in hot climates, where the loose, scrambling stems may need support. Pinch young growth to build fullness and cut back if it sprawls.

planting.note: Plant after frost in rich, warm soil.

directions: This showy rosebud sage forms a tall, bold shrub topped with fat clusters of bright magenta-pink flowers that open from round, bud-like tips. Grow from cuttings in fertile soil with steady moisture and a little afternoon shade in hot regions. Its main show comes in late summer and fall, so delay hard pruning until spring in mild zones.

planting.note: Set out after frost in warm, fertile ground.

directions: This dramatic Brazilian sage is grown for its large, rough leaves and long, densely packed spikes of orange-red flowers in rust-colored calyces. Grow from cuttings in rich soil and full sun, giving the tall stems shelter from strong wind. Flowering peaks in late summer and fall, and cuttings taken before frost carry it over winter.

planting.note: Plant after frost in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: This tall, striking sage bears deep cobalt-blue flowers set in showy chartreuse-green calyces that give the plant its name. Grow from cuttings in rich soil and full sun, allowing plenty of room for its large, upright frame. The main display comes in fall, so hold off on hard pruning until spring in mild-winter gardens.

planting.note: Set out after frost in a hot, dry spot.

directions: This bold subshrub forms a large mound of white-woolly, arrow-shaped leaves topped by branching sprays of magenta-pink flowers in papery calyces. Grow from seed or cuttings in full sun and sharply drained soil, and keep it dry once established. Trim after flowering and shelter it from cold, wet winters.

planting.note: Direct sow around the last frost date.

directions: The showy part of painted sage is the cluster of violet-blue leafy bracts, not the tiny true flowers, and this form gives the deepest color. Sow seed directly where plants are to grow and thin to a hand's width apart in full sun. The colorful bracts hold for weeks and dry beautifully for arrangements.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in warm soil.

directions: This vigorous hybrid carries true-blue flowers held in near-black calyces on a large, well-branched plant that blooms until frost. Grow from cuttings in full sun and fertile soil, watering during dry heat. It needs no deadheading and works equally well in large containers and borders where it draws hummingbirds.

planting.note: Set out after frost into warm soil.

directions: Bred for large flowers on an upright, well-behaved plant, this selection carries bright rose-pink tubular blooms from summer to frost. Grow from cuttings in full sun and fertile, well-drained soil, feeding lightly for best performance. It excels in containers and beds and keeps blooming without deadheading.

planting.note: Transplant in spring once the soil has warmed and settled.

directions: Prized for its creamy variegated foliage as much as its double soft-pink flowers, this shrub wants full sun to keep the leaf edges crisp. Plant at the same depth it grew in the container and mulch to hold moisture. The seedless double blooms appear from summer through fall without messy self-sowing.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost, spacing to allow air movement.

directions: This selection bears pure white anemone-form flowers with a lacy central skirt of extra petals over a long summer season. Give it fertile, well-drained soil and full sun for the heaviest flowering. Late-winter pruning keeps the shrub tidy and stimulates strong flowering shoots.

planting.note: Set out after the last spring frost in a sunny bed.

directions: Part of the Satin series, this shrub produces large single pink flowers with minimal seed set for cleaner summer color. Plant in full sun with the crown at grade and keep evenly moist the first season. Trim in early spring to maintain an upright, well-branched form.

planting.note: Plant in spring once frost danger has passed.

directions: Rich violet-purple single flowers with a red throat cover this vigorous shrub from midsummer to frost. Choose a full-sun site with good drainage and water regularly until roots take hold. Prune hard in late winter for the biggest flowers on the fresh season's growth.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost into a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: This nearly sterile selection opens orchid-pink flowers with a dramatic magenta-red eye and produces very little seed. Plant with the root ball level with the ground and mulch lightly. Expect bloom from summer into fall, and shape the plant with a late-winter pruning.

planting.note: Plant in spring; its narrow habit suits tight spaces and hedges.

directions: A strongly columnar shrub that grows tall and narrow, making it ideal for screens where width is limited. It carries semi-double white flowers along the upright stems through summer. Little pruning is needed beyond removing wayward or damaged branches in late winter.

planting.note: Set out in spring after frost in full sun.

directions: An old French cultivar bearing fully double, pure white flowers that resemble small peonies in late summer. Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and water through dry spells for consistent bloom. Thin crowded stems in late winter to keep the interior open and healthy.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny location with room to spread.

directions: This heirloom shrub produces showy double lavender-purple flowers over a long midsummer-to-fall season. Give it full sun and average, well-drained soil for the best display. Prune in late winter to control size and promote vigorous flowering wood.

planting.note: Plant in spring once frost has passed in full sun.

directions: An old cultivar valued for its single, violet-blue flowers accented with a reddish throat. Site it in full sun with well-drained soil and water steadily while young. Blooms carry from midsummer to frost, and pruning in late winter encourages robust new growth.

planting.note: Set out in spring after frost in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: This Chiffon-series shrub opens anemone-form flowers of soft pink streaked with raspberry rays radiating from the center. Plant in full sun for the fullest flowering and keep the soil evenly moist the first year. Prune in late winter to shape and to boost the size of the summer blooms.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted plants in spring after the last frost.

directions: This dinner-plate hibiscus pairs deep wine-red foliage with brilliant scarlet flowers up to nine inches across. Plant in full sun in rich, consistently moist soil, since it tolerates wet ground well. Cut the dead stems to the ground in early spring; it emerges late, so mark its spot to avoid disturbing the crown.

planting.note: Set out in spring after frost in full sun and moist soil.

directions: Compact and bushy, this selection covers itself in glowing cranberry-red flowers with a velvety sheen. Plant in full sun and keep the soil moist to wet for strongest performance. It breaks dormancy late in spring, so be patient before assuming winter loss, and cut old stems back before new shoots appear.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in full sun and fertile, moist soil.

directions: Large lavender-pink flowers with a ruffled edge and cherry-red eye smother this rounded plant in mid to late summer. Give it a sunny site with steady moisture and space for its wide mounding habit. Remove last year's stems in early spring and mark the location, as it is one of the last perennials to reappear.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in a sunny, evenly moist bed.

directions: This bold selection combines chocolate-infused dark green foliage with large pink flowers veined in cherry-red. Plant in full sun in rich soil that stays consistently moist for lush growth and heavy bloom. Cut stems to the ground in early spring and expect late emergence from the crown.

planting.note: Set out in spring after frost in full sun with ample moisture.

directions: Deep purple-black foliage sets off large rosy-pink flowers with a darker eye on this well-branched plant. Plant in full sun and keep soil moist to wet, as this hibiscus thrives near ponds and in low spots. Trim old growth to ground level in spring before the late-emerging shoots push up.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in a sunny, moist location.

directions: Large creamy pale-yellow flowers with a red center age to soft buttery tones on this vigorous, rounded plant. Give it full sun and reliably moist, fertile soil for the best flower size. Cut back to the ground in early spring, and mark the crown since it wakes up late in the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in full sun and moist soil.

directions: This selection is prized for its nearly black foliage paired with huge deep-red flowers for high contrast. Plant in full sun and keep the root zone consistently moist for the richest leaf color. Cut spent stems to the base in early spring and expect the crown to break dormancy late.

planting.note: Set out in spring after frost in full sun with steady moisture.

directions: A compact, wide-mounding plant with near-black foliage and pale pink flowers marked by a bold red eye. Its low, spreading habit suits smaller beds and the front of borders in full sun. Keep the soil moist to wet, cut stems down in spring, and be patient with its late emergence.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in a sunny, moist bed.

directions: Bicolor flowers blend rosy-pink and white in a pinwheel pattern around a red eye on this tidy, compact plant. Grow it in full sun with rich, moisture-retentive soil for dense flowering. Cut old stems to the ground in early spring and mark the spot, as new growth appears late.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in full sun and moist soil.

directions: Large lavender-pink flowers with fine crimson veining sit above deeply cut, coppery-purple maple-like foliage. Site it in full sun with consistently moist soil to support its large size and abundant bloom. Remove the previous year's stems in early spring and wait for the late-emerging crown.

planting.note: Set out in spring after frost in full sun with ample moisture.

directions: This compact selection bears bright candy-pink flowers with a red eye against dark-tinged green foliage. Plant in full sun in fertile, moisture-retentive soil for heavy summer flowering. Cut stems back in early spring and mark the crown, which emerges later than most perennials.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in a sunny, moist location.

directions: Enormous overlapping white-to-blush flowers with a small red eye give this plant a soft, elegant look. Grow in full sun with rich, evenly moist soil to fuel its dinner-plate blooms. Cut the old stems to the ground in early spring and expect late emergence from the crown.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in full sun and moist soil.

directions: Vivid true-red flowers stand out against dark bronze-green foliage on this upright, well-branched plant. Plant in full sun in consistently moist soil, and it will also handle boggy sites. Remove last season's stems in early spring and mark the crown so you don't disturb the late shoots.

planting.note: Set out in spring after frost in full sun with steady moisture.

directions: Deep magenta-purple flowers with a darker eye glow above dark green foliage on this bushy plant. Grow in full sun in rich, moist soil for the fullest flowering through late summer. Cut stems to the ground in early spring and be patient, as this hibiscus emerges late.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in full sun and moist ground.

directions: Named for its striking near-black leaves speckled with lighter veins, this plant carries soft pink flowers with a deep eye. Site it in full sun and keep the soil moist to wet for the darkest foliage and best bloom. Cut old stems down in spring and mark the crown, as it reappears late.

common_name: Turn of the Century Hardy Hibiscus

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in full sun with abundant moisture.

directions: This tall selection is famous for pinwheel flowers striped in pink and white, with each bloom unique. Give it full sun and rich, consistently moist soil to support its large stature and long bloom. Cut back to the ground in early spring and mark its location, since it is slow to reappear.

planting.note: Sow scarified seed or set plants in spring at a pond edge or wet bed.

directions: A large native rosemallow of southeastern wetlands, it bears fragrant pale-pink flowers with a red center and grayish, felted maple-like leaves. Plant in full sun in wet to constantly moist soil, ideal along ponds and rain gardens. Scarify seed before sowing, and cut the tall stems to the ground after frost kills the top growth.

planting.note: Grow in containers and move outdoors only after nights stay warm.

directions: This tropical shrub bears distinctive pendant flowers with deeply fringed, recurved coral-red petals dangling on slender stalks. Root it from softwood cuttings and grow in rich, well-drained soil with steady warmth and moisture. In cold climates keep it in a pot to overwinter indoors, and prune lightly to maintain a graceful arching shape.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Sow seed on the soil surface indoors 8 to 10 weeks before your last frost, pressing lightly without covering since light aids sprouting. Move hardened plants outdoors after frost and space well for their vigorous spreading habit. Feed regularly and shear lightly midseason to keep the sky-blue flowers coming until fall.

planting.note: Transplant after frost danger passes into warm ground.

directions: Start seed indoors on the surface of moist mix and keep warm and bright until seedlings establish. This hedge-type petunia grows tall where crowded and low where given room, so adjust spacing to the effect you want. Water at the base and feed steadily to sustain the cherry-rose blooms through the heat of summer.

planting.note: Plant out after the last spring frost.

directions: Surface-sow indoors under bright light 8 to 10 weeks ahead of transplanting and do not bury the tiny seed. Give this robust spreader room and full sun, where its deep velvety-red flowers hold color even in strong heat. Keep evenly moist and fertilize through the season for continuous bloom.

planting.note: Move outdoors once frost has passed.

directions: Sow on the surface of a warm, moist seed mix and leave uncovered for light. This small-flowered spreading petunia opens lavender-blue blooms that fade toward soft white, giving a changing denim wash across the plant. Plant in baskets or beds, feed regularly, and no deadheading is needed for steady flowering.

planting.note: Transplant after the danger of frost is over.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed indoors and keep warm and bright until seedlings are stout enough to handle. The original ground-hugging Wave petunia trails widely, so give it space or let it cascade from containers. Provide full sun and steady feeding, and its magenta-purple carpet will bloom nonstop through summer.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost date.

directions: Start seed indoors on the surface of moist mix 8 to 10 weeks before planting out. This mounding grandiflora carries large deep blackberry-purple flowers on a tidy, well-branched plant. Grow in sun with even moisture and light feeding, pinching young plants once to encourage bushy growth.

planting.note: Plant out when frost has passed.

directions: Sow the tiny seed on the surface of a warm mix and leave uncovered for light. This milliflora type makes a dense mound of small violet-blue flowers that need no deadheading. Ideal for small pots and edging, it wants full sun, even moisture, and regular light feeding.

planting.note: Transplant after the final frost of spring.

directions: Surface-sow indoors and keep bright and warm until seedlings develop. This award-winning petunia blankets itself in clear deep-yellow flowers on a mounding, trailing plant. Grow in full sun with steady moisture and feeding for a solid ball of golden bloom all season.

planting.note: Move plants outdoors after frost.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist mix indoors about 10 weeks before planting out and provide light and warmth. Uncommon among petunias for its true deep-orange color, it mounds and trails freely in beds and baskets. Give full sun, even water, and regular feeding for reliable summer-long color.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Surface-sow indoors under light and keep the mix warm until germination. Prized for its rich evening fragrance, this petunia bears indigo-blue flowers on a spreading, basket-friendly plant. Place it near seating in full sun, keep evenly moist, and feed regularly for scent and color together.

planting.note: Plant purchased starts out after frost has passed.

directions: Grown from cuttings rather than seed, set nursery plants out after frost in rich, well-drained soil or containers. This vigorous spreader shows soft violet-pink flowers veined with deep plum and needs no deadheading. Water consistently and feed often, shearing lightly if stems stretch to renew fresh bloom.

planting.note: Set out cutting-grown plants after the last frost.

directions: Plant vegetatively propagated starts after frost in full sun with fertile, freely draining soil. Flowers open rich raspberry-pink with a darker throat on a mounding, trailing plant that self-cleans. Keep evenly moist, feed regularly, and trim wayward stems midsummer to keep the mound full.

planting.note: Transplant nursery starts after frost passes.

directions: Set out cutting-grown plants after frost into full sun and rich, well-drained soil. Its purple flowers are edged in a distinctive lime-green rim that brightens mixed containers. Water steadily and feed frequently, and shear back lightly if growth thins to restore dense bloom.

planting.note: Plant out after the last spring frost.

directions: Plant vegetative starts after frost in a sunny spot with fertile, well-drained soil or potting mix. Deep wine-red flowers with a near-black center give this trailing petunia a velvety, saturated look. Keep the soil evenly moist, feed often, and clip back leggy stems to keep the plant compact and blooming.

planting.note: Set out plants once frost danger passes.

directions: Grown from cuttings, plant starts after frost in full sun with rich, quick-draining soil. This smaller-flowered, well-branched selection carries masses of deep indigo-blue blooms on a mounding, spreading plant. Water evenly, feed regularly, and no deadheading is needed for steady color into fall.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into warm soil.

directions: Set out cutting-grown plants after frost in full sun and fertile, well-drained soil. Blooms open in warm blends of soft peach, coral, and yellow that shift with the light and season. Water consistently and feed often, trimming lightly midseason to keep the trailing plant dense.

planting.note: Plant nursery starts out after the last frost.

directions: Plant cutting-grown starts after frost in full sun with rich, well-drained soil. This mounding petunia bears near-black, velvety deep-purple flowers that make a striking container centerpiece. Water evenly and feed regularly, pinching early growth to build a bushy, free-flowering plant.

planting.note: Set out after frost danger has passed.

directions: Grown from cuttings, plant starts after frost in full sun and fertile, well-drained mix. Magenta flowers carry a crisp lighter rim on a trailing plant well suited to baskets and window boxes. Water steadily, feed regularly, and shear lightly midsummer to keep the cascade full and blooming.

planting.note: Transplant nursery starts after the last frost.

directions: Set out cutting-grown plants after frost in full sun and rich, well-drained soil. Each yellow flower is marked with a pattern of red hearts around the center, a novelty that reads best up close. Water evenly and feed often, trimming spent growth lightly to keep the patterned blooms coming.

planting.note: Plant cutting-grown starts after frost.

directions: Calibrachoa is grown from cuttings, so set nursery plants out after frost in full sun and slightly acidic, well-drained mix. Its small petunia-like flowers cover a trailing plant in true violet-blue without any deadheading. Let the soil dry slightly between waterings and feed regularly, as this genus is a heavy feeder.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost.

directions: Plant vegetative starts after frost in full sun and acidic, freely draining soil. Clean white flowers with a yellow eye cover the trailing stems and need no deadheading. Water when the surface dries and feed regularly with a fertilizer for acid-loving plants to keep foliage green and bloom heavy.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into warm soil.

directions: Set out cutting-grown plants after frost in full sun with acidic, well-drained mix. Coral-pink flowers carry a pale star pattern radiating from a yellow throat on a trailing, self-cleaning plant. Let the mix dry slightly between waterings and feed regularly to sustain steady bloom.

planting.note: Plant out after the last spring frost.

directions: Plant nursery starts after frost in full sun and acidic, well-drained soil or containers. Warm coral-orange flowers age to softer peach, giving the trailing plant a creamsicle glow. Water when the surface dries, feed regularly, and no deadheading is required for continuous color.

planting.note: Set out plants once frost has passed.

directions: Plant cutting-grown starts after frost in full sun and acidic, well-drained mix. This selection carries fully double, rosette-like flowers in deep blue-purple on a trailing plant. Let soil dry slightly between waterings and feed regularly, as the extra petals demand steady nutrition.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost date.

directions: Set out vegetative starts after frost in full sun and acidic, freely draining soil. Double ruby-red rosette flowers cover the trailing stems for a rich, mini-rose effect in baskets. Water when the surface dries, feed regularly, and trim lightly if stems thin to renew bloom.

planting.note: Plant out after frost has passed.

directions: Plant nursery starts after frost in full sun and acidic, well-drained mix. Yellow flowers are veined and washed with red in a marbled pattern that shifts across the plant. Let the soil dry slightly between waterings, feed regularly, and leave the self-cleaning blooms to fall on their own.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost.

directions: Plant cutting-grown starts after frost in full sun and acidic, well-drained soil. Flowers blend coral, rose, and yellow in a sunrise pattern that varies bloom to bloom. Water when the surface dries and feed regularly with acid-forming fertilizer for lush growth and heavy bloom.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into warm soil.

directions: Set out vegetative starts after frost in full sun and acidic, well-drained mix. This selection produces fully double pink flowers on a compact, well-branched mounding plant. Let the soil dry slightly between waterings and feed regularly to support the petal-packed blooms.

planting.note: Plant out after the last spring frost.

directions: Plant nursery starts after frost in full sun and acidic, freely draining soil. This vigorous series holds bright hot-orange flowers well even in summer heat and rain. Water when the surface dries, feed regularly, and enjoy the self-cleaning blooms without deadheading.

planting.note: Set out plants once frost danger passes.

directions: Plant cutting-grown starts after frost in full sun and acidic, well-drained mix. This mounding, semi-trailing selection carries saturated deep-blue flowers on tidy plants ideal for combinations. Let the soil dry slightly between waterings and feed regularly to keep the color rich.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost date.

directions: Set out vegetative starts after frost in full sun and acidic, well-drained soil. This compact, mounding series bears rose-pink flowers and branches freely without pinching. Water when the surface dries, feed regularly, and the small blooms will cover the plant all season.

planting.note: Move plants outdoors after the last frost.

directions: Start seed indoors in darkness and be patient, as germination is uneven over several weeks. This low, spreading verbena forms a mat studded with clusters of brilliant scarlet-red flowers. Grow in full sun and lean, well-drained soil, watering sparingly once established since it resents wet feet.

planting.note: Transplant after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow indoors and cover lightly, keeping the mix warm; germination is slow and staggered. Named for its finely cut, ferny foliage, this trailing verbena carries flat clusters of lavender to purple flowers. Give it full sun and sharply drained soil, and water only in dry spells once it is rooted in.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost.

directions: Start seed indoors and cover lightly; expect slow, uneven germination over a few weeks. This native trailing verbena spreads into a low mat topped with rose-pink to lavender flower clusters. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, water sparingly once established, and shear after flushes to keep it tidy.

planting.note: Plant cutting-grown starts after frost.

directions: Grown from cuttings, set nursery plants out after frost in full sun and well-drained soil. Large flower clusters open in a bicolor blend of soft rose and white on a spreading, mildew-resistant plant. Water at the base to keep foliage dry, feed regularly, and trim lightly to encourage repeat bloom.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost.

directions: Plant vegetative starts after frost in full sun and well-drained soil or containers. Pale lavender flowers are streaked with darker purple veining across large, showy clusters. Water at the soil line to reduce mildew, feed regularly, and shear spent heads to keep flowering strong.
